perturbation.original_text: (d) Independent Contractor. Neither party shall, for any purpose, be deemed ---------------------- to be an agent of the other party and the relationship between the parties shall only be that of independent contractors. Neither party shall have any right or authority to assume or create any obligations or to make any representations or warranties on behalf of any other party, whether express or implied, or to bind the other party in any respect whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) Independent Contractor. Neither party shall, for any purpose, be deemed ---------------------- to be an agent of the other party; however, the relationship between the parties shall be that of joint venturers. Either party shall have the right or authority to assume or create obligations and to make representations or warranties on behalf of any other party, whether express or implied, or to bind the other party in any respect whatsoever, with prior written consent.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the relationship from "independent contractors" to "joint venturers" creates a contradiction. While the agreement intends to establish independence, granting authority to bind the other party, even with consent, implies a level of control and shared liability inconsistent with an independent contractor relationship. This can lead to confusion regarding liability for taxes, debts, and legal obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the common law and IRS guidelines on distinguishing between independent contractors and joint ventures. By granting each party the ability to bind the other, even with written consent, the agreement implies a degree of control and shared risk typical of a joint venture, not an independent contractor. This misclassification can have significant tax and legal ramifications.

Definitions (a) When used in this title, where not otherwise distinctly expressed or manifestly incompatible with the intent thereof- (1) Person The term "person" shall be construed to mean and include an individual, a trust, estate, partnership, association, company or corporation. (2) Partnership and partner The term "partnership" includes a syndicate, group, pool, joint venture, or other unincorporate
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original intent of establishing an independent contractor relationship by introducing elements of a joint venture. The original text explicitly states that neither party can bind the other. However, the changed text grants each party the authority to bind the other with prior written consent, altering the nature of the relationship. Snippet 1, 26 USC 7701, defines 'partnership' to include a 'joint venture'. This shift implies a shared risk and control level inconsistent with an independent contractor relationship, as highlighted in the Perturbation and Law explanations. The introduction of 'joint venturers' and the ability to bind each other contradicts the 'independent contractors' provision, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (i) Notices. Any notice required or permitted by this Agreement shall be in ------- writing and shall be deemed sufficient upon receipt, when delivered personally or by courier, overnight delivery service or confirmed facsimile, or forty-eight (48) hours after being deposited in the regular mail as certified or registered mail (airmail if sent internationally) with postage prepaid, if such notice is addressed to the party to be notified at such party's address or facsimile number as set forth below, or as subsequently modified by written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: (i) Notices. Any notice required or permitted by this Agreement shall be in ------- writing and shall be deemed sufficient when sent by telepathy, smoke signal, or carrier pigeon. If these methods are unavailable, notices may be sent by regular mail, without requiring receipt confirmation.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces absurd and legally unsound methods of notice (telepathy, smoke signal, carrier pigeon) and removes the requirement for receipt confirmation when using regular mail. This contradicts established legal standards for proper notification, creating uncertainty about whether notice was actually received.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the FRCP Rule 5(b) as the rule defines requirements for serving pleadings and other papers, which include delivery in a manner that is reasonably likely to be received. Telepathy, smoke signals, and carrier pigeons are not reliable means of communication, and removing the requirement for receipt confirmation for regular mail makes it difficult to prove that notice was properly given, conflicting with legal standards of due process.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 5. Serving and Filing Pleadings and Other Papers | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 5. Serving and Filing Pleadings and Other Papers (a) Service: When Required. (1) In General. Unless these rules provide otherwise, each of the following papers must be served on every party: (A) an order stating that service is required; (B) a pleading filed after the original complaint, unless the court orders otherwise under Rule 5(c) because there are numerous defendants; (C) a discovery paper required to be served on a party, unless the court orders otherwise; (D) a written motion, except one that may be heard ex parte; and (E) a written notice, appearance, demand, or offer of judgment, or any similar paper. (2) If a Party Fails to Appear. No service is required on a party who is in default for failing to appear. But a pleading that asserts a new claim for relief against such a party must be served on that party under Rule 4 . (3) Seizing Property. If an action is begun by seizing property and no person is or need be named as a defendant, any service required before the filing of an appearance, answer, or claim must be made on the person who had custody or possession of the property when it was seized. (b) Service: How Made. (1) Serving an Attorney. If a party is represented by an attorney, service under this rule must be made on the attorney unless the court orders service on the party. (2) Service in General. A paper is served under this rule by: (A) handing it to the person; (B) leaving it: (i) at the person's office with a clerk or other person in charge or, if no one is in charge, in a conspicuous place in the office; or (ii) if the person has no office or the office is closed, at the person's dwelling or usual place of abode with someone of suitable age and discretion who resides there; (C) mailing it to the person's last known address—in which event service is complete upon mai

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces methods of notification (telepathy, smoke signals, carrier pigeon) that are unreliable and not reasonably likely to be received. This directly conflicts with the requirement for proper service as implied by FRCP Rule 5(b), which necessitates delivery in a manner reasonably likely to be received. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that papers must be served, implicitly requiring a reliable method to ensure the recipient receives them. The introduction of these absurd methods, combined with the removal of receipt confirmation for regular mail, makes proving that notice was properly given difficult, directly contradicting legal standards of due process.

perturbation.original_text: (f) Governing Law. This Agreement and all acts and transactions pursuant ------------- hereto and the rights and obligations of the parties hereto shall be governed, construed and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without giving effect to principles of conflicts of law.
perturbation.changed_text: (f) Governing Law. This Agreement and all acts and transactions pursuant ------------- hereto and the rights and obligations of the parties hereto shall be governed, construed and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of California, but only if interpreted by the legal standards of Narnia.
perturbation.explanation: By stipulating that California law is applicable only if interpreted by "legal standards of Narnia," the modification introduces a fantastical and undefined element that renders the governing law clause ambiguous and unenforceable. There are no recognized 'legal standards of Narnia', and incorporating such language directly conflicts with the purpose of a governing law clause, which is to provide a clear and predictable legal framework.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts California Civil Code § 1636, which requires contracts to be interpreted in a way that gives effect to the mutual intention of the parties. The reference to "legal standards of Narnia" introduces ambiguity and prevents a clear determination of the parties' intent, undermining the enforceability of the contract under California law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1636. A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces 'legal standards of Narnia,' which contradicts California Civil Code § 1636. The original clause intended to establish California law as the governing law. By adding the Narnia clause, the text undermines the enforceability of the agreement under California law because there is no clear way to determine the parties' intent as required by CIV 1636: 'A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful.' The added text renders the 'mutual intention' unascertainable.
